Study of trigonometric functions, graphs, formulas and identities; inverse functions, including inverse trigonometric functions; solutions to systems of equations; and polar coordinate systems.

title: Discrete Mathematics
description: A study of discrete structures geared toward those entering computer or electrical engineering. Topics may include problem solving, numeration systems, sets, logic, relations, recursion, graphs and trees, sorting, Boolean algebra and circuits, and elementary data structures.

title: Portfolio Assessment II
description: This senior presentation course is the culmination of your studies, meant to help you demonstrate talent, skills and presentation, and communication abilities. This experience will not only provide you the opportunity to begin honing your skills in presenting your work to be used for expanding your career, but it will allow you to receive feedback from experienced designers and developers from the industry on ways you can improve your portfolio as you begin your job hunt. While it is critical to show finished work which reflects strong concepts, design and technical abilities, it is also important to be able to demonstrate the process used to create the work along with the reasoning which led its final form. Each senior is allowed to present 4- 5 projects. These projects can have multiple components but, in total, can't exceed a total of 10. Presentations are to be, at least, 10 minutes and no longer than 15 and are to be made digitally online or with a projector. The presentation should cover your creative process from beginning to end.

title: Calculus II
description: A study of Cartesian and polar coordinates, parametric equations, vectors and vector-valued functions in 2 and 3 dimensions, limits, differentiation of functions with applications, integration of functions with applications, Taylor polynomials, and series. It is strongly recommended that students take PHY 2110 and PHY 2120 concurrently with MAT 1510 and MAT 1520, respectively.

title: Perspectives in Mathematics
description: An introduction to mathematical thought for students, especially freshmen, who are interested in a mathematics major or minor, or for any student interested in learning about some important ideas and uses of mathematics. Several contemporary mathematical topics will be studied, such as recently solved problems, orders of infinity, Fermat's last theorem, Tangles and knot theory, cryptography, the fourth dimension, the four-color theorem, topology, or fractals and chaos. Graded S, U.

title: Calculus I
description: A study of Cartesian and polar coordinates, parametric equations, vectors and vector-valued functions in 2 and 3 dimensions, limits, differentiation of functions with applications, integration of functions with applications, Taylor polynomials, and series. It is strongly recommended that students take PHY 2110 and PHY 2120 concurrently with MAT 1510 and MAT 1520, respectively.

title: Statistical Analysis for the Sciences
description: An introductory statistics course with an emphasis on exploratory and inferential techniques for analyzing and modeling data. Topics include descriptive statistics, regression, probability distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing and experiment design. Statistical software is implemented throughout the course.

title: Adventure Based Leadership
description: Provides students in Trevecca’s Leadership Minor with an introduction to adventure education in addition to training and certification in group facilitation. The curriculum is designed to engage students in the historical and theoretical elements of adventure education as well as experiential learning. This experiential learning takes place through earning a certificate in First Aid, CPR, and AED as well as ropes course facilitation for the purpose of applying that to leadership development and team building. Students who participate in the class will be able to apply these leadership skills on campus, in the surrounding community and beyond.

title: Destructive Leaders and Dysfunctional Culture
description: Leadership is one of the most studied concepts within the social sciences. More often than not, we look at what leaders are doing right. This class allows learners to understand leadership more deeply by studying the opposite spectrum of effective leadership. We live in a society that provides countless examples of failed leadership in politics, religion, athletics, etc. Why do so many leaders fall? Learners will be exposed to theoretical principles and research on the dysfunctional side of leadership. In a nutshell, this course is the "Abnormal Psychology Class" for leadership.

title: Calculus III
description: A study of partial derivatives and multiple integrals and additional topics in vector-valued functions.

title: Theory of Probability and Mathematical Statistics
description: Probability of sample spaces, random variables, discrete and continuous probability distributions. Analysis of the principles of descriptive and inferential statistics.

title: Introduction to Mathematical Modeling
description: Introduction to the basics of mathematical modeling, emphasizing model construction, analysis and application. Using examples from a variety of fields such as physics, biology, chemistry, business, economics, and other non-mathematical disciplines, students will learn how to develop and use both discrete and continuous mathematical models of real-world systems. Computer software will be used, but primary emphasis will be placed on achieving a mental understanding of the relationship described within the models.

title: Introduction to Abstract Mathematics
description: Study of basic ideas and methods of mathematics including sets, logic, proof techniques, relations, functions, mathematical induction, and modular arithmetic. Prerequisite or corequisite: MAT 1520.

title: Discrete Methods
description: Discrete mathematical ideas including graph theory, counting techniques, recurrence relations, and methods of solving applied problems.

title: Linear and Matrix Algebra
description: Linear equations, vector operations, matrices, determinants, linear transformations, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, singular values and singular vectors, canonical forms, polynomial functions of matrices, vector spaces, and related topics.

title: Junior Seminar in Mathematics
description: Requires students to participate in professional development activities designed to prepare them to apply for summer research/internship positions, graduate school, and/or jobs in mathematical, scientific or technical fields. In addition, students will explore the integration of faith with their desired profession.

title: Research/Creative Project Proposal
description: Development of an individualized original research or creative scholarship project under the mentorship of a faculty member. Tasks will include crafting the proposal, preparing an appropriate review of literature or other background information for the project, and outlining the methods by which these student plans to conduct the project. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course.

title: Bioinformatics
description: Introduces the scientist to Bioinformatics, which uses computer databases to store, retrieve and assist in understanding biological information. Hands-on sessions will familiarize students with the details and use of the most commonly used online tools and resources.

title: Faculty-Led Academic Research Experiences (FLARE)
description: Intensive participation as a team member in the completion of a scholarly research or creative project. A faculty member will lead each project and will mentor students throughout the project. Enrollment is limited; application is required, and students are selected by the faculty member leading the project. For more information, see the Quality Enhancement Plan section of the Catalog.

title: Mathematical Methods for Physical Science and Engineering
description: Integrates concepts of linear algebra and differential equations that are used in upper-level engineering and physical science courses. Course content includes complex variables, linear algebra, inner product spaces, Fourier series and transforms, ordinary and partial differential equations, Laplace transforms, and special functions. Lecture

title: Research/Creative Project Completion
description: Successful execution of an individualized research or creative scholarship project under the mentorship of a faculty member and consistent with practices in his or her discipline. Students will be evaluated on their adherence to approved methodology, logical development of the project, and time management, as well as their project progress. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course. May be retaken for additional credit if the scope of the research project justifies it.

title: Introduction to Real Analysis
description: Real number system, functions, sequences, limits, continuity, uniform continuity, infinite series and sequences. Prerequisite or corequisite: MAT 2510 and MAT 2600.

title: Modern Algebra
description: Sets, groups, rings, and fields. Logical rigorous proofs and analysis are emphasized.

title: Research/Create Project Presentation
description: Revision and presentation of research findings or creative scholarship project developed in MAT 499A & MAT 499B.  Presentation may be submission for publication, oral presentation, or performance for a specified public audience. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course.

title: Introduction to Operations Research
description: Study of methods and ideas in operations research including linear programming using the simplex method and interior point methods, nonlinear programming, stochastic processes and Markov chains.

title: Euclidean and Non-Euclidean Geometries
description: A study of plane geometry based on Euclid's axioms, both with and without the parallel postulate. The course includes formal study of the consequences of these axioms, transformational geometry, and algebraic models of both Euclidean and non-Euclidean systems.

title: Topics in Mathematics
description: In-depth coverage of one topic to be selected from pure or applied mathematics, with regard to interest of the instructor and students. Possible topics include combinatorics, number theory, differential geometry, topology, numerical analysis, coding theory, cryptology, partial differential equations, Euclidean and non-Euclidean geometry, probability, or statistics. Permission of the instructor required. May be repeated for credit.

title: Differential Equations
description: An introduction to ordinary differential equations, including linear and nonlinear equations with applications, power series solutions, and Laplace transforms.

title: Directed Study in Math
description: For advanced students working on special projects supervised by the instructor.

title: Seminar in Mathematics
description: Projects and paper presented to the mathematics faculty and other mathematics majors. To be taken by seniors.

title: Career Internship in Mathematics
description: Mathematics service laboratory (on-the-job experience) under the supervision of faculty. Juniors and seniors only. Maximum 6 hrs. Graded S/U.

title: Foundations of Music Production
description: This course provides the student with foundational principles for music productions. The student will develop a comprehensive understanding of planning, executing, and leading through the phases of various music production models.

title: Commercial Music Business
description: This course provides an introduction to the broad spectrum of commercial music as a business. Students are introduced to an array of commercial music career opportunities and avenues for generating income through music.  Assignments involve practical application of the process of creating and monetizing music through various avenues, including: recorded music, publishing, live performance, video production, and more.

title: Class Piano II
description: A continuation of MCM 1600.  Beginning class piano for commercial music majors.  Areas of study will include: (1) Aural transcription and dictation, (2) Scales and modes, (3) Harmonization by sight and ear, (4) Major and minor chord progressions, (5) Development of reading skills, and (6) Improvisation.

title: Commercial Music Style & Studio Musicianship I
description: This foundational course introduces genre specific performance technique. Students are equipped to identify and perform a broad spectrum of commercial music repertoire.  In addition, students study and apply acceptable studio etiquette, protocol, and practice.

title: Commercial Music Style & Studio Musicianship II
description: This class is a continuation of MCM 1665.  Emphasis will be placed on advanced techniques essential for professional studio recording and production.

title: Elective Private Piano
description: Elective private study offers comprehensive instruction of performance techniques, emphasizing the cultivation of technical prowess and musical proficiency through the study of appropriate repertoire.

Adaptive tools will be used to help students with limited musical backgrounds bring their ideas to life (i.e. Guitar App or co-writing).

title: Commercial Ensemble
description: Performs a diverse range of music from Classic Rock to Contemporary Christian music on amplified instruments. This group is open to all musicians, vocalists, instrumentalists, and rhythm players.

title: Commercial Theory
description: Chord progressions, the Nashville number system, song forms, harmonic techniques, listening skills, and charting are all addressed in this class.

title: Introduction to Music Leadership
description: In this course, students learn how to design and implement practical organization management strategies for music leadership, develop strategic planning and decision-making ability, and cultivate an efficient and practical problem-solving skill set. Practical application is made to various music leadership environments and active job placement opportunities.

title: Element
description: Auditioned, guitar-driven, rock, fusion, and pop ensemble that explores advanced repertoire.

title: The Home Studio
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the setup, operation, and creative use of home recording studios. Students learn the fundamental principles of audio recording, mixing, and production in a home studio environment. By the end of the course, students will be equipped to set up and operate a home studio for commercial music production, from tracking, final mix to mastering.

title: History of Songwriting
description: An introduction to the craft, development and history of songwriting from 1820 forward. Attention is given to the study and evaluation of song form, both historic and contemporary. General techniques of songwriting and the specific approaches of selected songwriters are explored in connection with biblical doctrine and theologies, music practices, and worship practices in the Christian community.

title: History of the Christian Music Artist
description: This is a historical overview of The Christian Music Artist’s role in various evangelical church traditions from the days of William Bradbury and the Sunday School Awakening, Ira Sankey and the New York Prayer Revival, Homer Rodeheaver and The Billy Sunday Crusades, George Beverly Shea and The Billy Graham Revivals through contemporary musicians, such as Kate Smith, Mahalia Jackson, Andraé Crouch, Bill Gaither, Ricky Scaggs, Michael W. Smith, Chris Tomlin, and others.

title: Digital Audio Workstation
description: This course explores the digital audio workstation environment through an overview of digital audio concepts and practices. Students will learn fundamental concepts of digital audio workstations (DAWs), particularly Logic Pro while developing practical skills with professional recording equipment. The curriculum covers DAW hardware/software integration and essential editing and production techniques in modern recording studios.

title: Recording Techniques
description: This course covers basic digital audio workstation principles. Students are introduced to an array of contemporary audio software platforms such as: Pro Tools, Cubase/Nuendo, Logic, PreSonus, and Digital Performer. Students will complete a digital audio project from initial setup to final mix-down.  Further topics covered: recording live audio, midi sequencing, software synthesizers, and audio editing (or region looping).

title: Editing and Mixing Techniques
description: This course provides students, comprehensive training in contemporary digital audio editing and mixing techniques using professional DAWs, like Logic Pro and Pro Tools. Students will develop proficiency in industry-standard software while learning the artistic and technical aspects of modern music production. Throughout the semester, students will explore advanced vocal tuning plugins and mixing tools. The course emphasizes both technical expertise and creative decision-making in the production process. Students will learn to critically analyze commercial productions and apply professional techniques to their own projects.

title: Improvisation I
description: Deals with the fundamentals of improvisation, including the study of harmonic changes, melodic creation, and personal expression through music. Open to vocalists and instrumentalists.

title: Improvisation II
description: The continued study of improvisation techniques.

title: Fuze
description: Auditioned, guitar-driven, rock, fusion, and pop ensemble that explores advanced repertoire.

title: Introduction to Commercial Music
description: This course is an exploration of the various roles, scope of application, career opportunities and income streams related to Commercial Music.  Attention is given to articulation and identification of the tools, skill set, and ethical practices needed or success in various music industry settings.

title: Creating Music For TV
description: "Music for TV" is designed to introduce students to the role of music in television production. The course will explore the creative, technical, and practical aspects of composing, producing, and synchronizing music for television, with a focus on the unique demands and styles that define TV scoring. Students will study the evolution of TV music, from traditional themes to modern episodic scores, and gain hands-on experience with the technologies and techniques used in composing for the medium.

title: Commercial Music History
description: A survey of the development of commercial music written in America from the early 19th century to the present. The term commercial refers to music written for consumable use and not primarily as a work of art.

title: History of Audio Recording
description: Designed to familiarize students with a historical account of recording audio spanning from the 19th century to present. Topics include: recording devices, styles, methods, and historical figures who shaped the art of recording.

title: Innovation in the Ensemble Arts
description: This course provides commercial music students opportunity to develop the necessary skill for building thriving music organizations through innovation and collaboration.  Students discuss various approaches music professionals have in applying individual, personal music creativity to ensemble development through “transformative renewal."

title: Songwriting I
description: Students will be introduced to the fundamental building blocks of songwriting: lyric, melody, harmony, form, voice, resource, and inspiration. Students will begin to cultivate a portfolio of original songs and will share them during routine class songwriter showcase gatherings.

title: Jazz History
description: Study of the historical development of Jazz as a commercial and artistic genre.

Students will continue to cultivate a portfolio of original songs and will share them during on-campus songwriter showcase gatherings.

title: The Working Musician
description: A course dealing with musicianship as a vocation. The goal is to expose the student to the professional expectations of the recording studio environment, touring, symphonic performance, and church musician. Topics will include arranging, the 'Nashville Number System,' contracts, union membership, wages, tax implications, career opportunities, and planning. Included will be recording sessions, field trips, and guest professionals from various areas of the music industry.

title: Pro Tools Techniques
description: Students learn to use cutting-edge technology when creating premium quality audio mixes. Utilizing industry standard tools, students apply production and mixing skills to live analog or digital console mixing projects.

title: Music Technology
description: Designed to familiarize students with current technology in the field of music by creating projects for aspects of audio, music notation software, and MIDI. Electronic composition shall be a primary component to facilitate technological learning.

title: Advanced Mixing and Post-Production
description: This course is a continuation of MCM 3200 Audio Production, Mixing and Engineering. Special attention is given to developing advanced skill in audio mixing, mastering, and editing technique.

title: Commercial Music Studio Pedagogy
description: This course deals with Commercial Music Studio Pedagogy and is designed to address the skills needed to perform commercial styles in various recording studio settings. In addition to becoming fully aware of which characteristics distinguish various commercial music styles from other styles, students become familiar with pedagogical approaches for vocal and instrumental recording performance technique. Opportunity is provided for students to participate in a number of actual recording experiences, including on-location observation and evaluation in various on-campus and Nashville venues.

title: Artist Management
description: Whether you aspire to be an artist manager or simply self-manage your musical career, this course provides a thorough, yet practical overview of what artist managers do, how to monetize key income streams, and how to build and execute a viable artist career plan.

title: Private Viola
description: Lower Division applied study offers comprehensive instruction of performance techniques, emphasizing the cultivation of technical prowess and musical proficiency through the study of appropriate repertoire.

title: Entrepreneurship in Commercial Music
description: A study of planning, management, and financing of small business venture.

title: Private Guitar
description: Lower Division applied study offers comprehensive instruction of performance techniques, emphasizing the cultivation of technical prowess and musical proficiency through the study of appropriate repertoire.

title: Mentoring in the Ensemble Arts
description: This course provides opportunity for students to develop mentoring concepts and skill necessary to excel as a leader of music. Students will learn about the reciprocal roles between mentor and mentee. Emphasis is given to the on- going responsibility of the music leader to mentor while engaging in various performance or job-related activities. The student will develop a “mentoring resume” to be included in the degree portfolio.

title: Faculty-Led Academic Research Experiences (FLARE)
description: Intensive participation as a team member in the completion of a scholarly research or creative project. A faculty member will lead each project and will mentor students throughout the project. Enrollment is limited; application is required, and students are selected by the faculty member leading the project. For more information, see the Quality Enhancement Plan section of the Catalog.

title: Writing Songs of Faith
description: Continued studies on advanced principles of songwriting, including: elements of writing melody; melodic structure; lyric form-understanding verse/chorus /bridge structures; melodic style; lyric style - gospel to classical, southern gospel to praise and worship and country to rock 'n roll; and co-writing lyrics with other musicians. Special attention is given to writing songs for specific performance purposes.  Students are required to participate in the Songwriter's Showcase presented by the SOMWA each semester.

title: Artist Presentation & Repertoire: The Concert
description: Developing strategies for musically artistic and ministry effective program presentation. Principles include building live performance, developing goals and objectives for platform performance; unique features of lighting, sound reinforcement, and technology; trend awareness and commerciality. Special emphasis is placed on goals of an artist as a worshiper; song selection; audience participation; presentation of the gospel; and partnership with the local church.

title: Software Integration in Music
description: Course description: This course explores the intersection of music, technology, and software engineering, equipping students with the skills to integrate diverse software tools in the creation, production, and performance of music. Topics include software integration, real-time processing, artificial intelligence in music and the assimilation of music with visual and multimedia environments. Students will also examine the technical and artistic challenges of software integration in both studio and live performance contexts.

title: Leadership Principles for the Commercial Musician
description: This course identifies and explores leadership principles unique to the field of music. Emphasis is given to the music leader’s responsibility to build team, identify strategic leadership roles in the music industry, cultivate communication skill, develop short- and long-term strategy, resolve conflict and to always function as a music professional. A capstone project to be included in the student’s portfolio is required.

title: Live Media Production
description: This hands-on course introduces students to the technical and creative aspects of live media production, focusing on professional audio techniques for concerts, live events, and broadcasts. Students will develop practical skills in setting up and operating professional sound systems while learning the fundamental concepts that inform production decisions. By the end of this course, students will be able to configure and operate professional audio equipment, create effective monitor and front-of-house mixes, implement digital audio networking using Dante, program digital mixing consoles, apply principles of acoustics to optimize sound in various venues, execute broadcast-quality mixes, and capture multi-track recordings of live performances.

title: Community Arts Innovation and Sustainability
description: The ability to maintain a consistent level of relevance is the goal of every arts organization.  To do so is to achieve sustainability.  Sustainability is directly related to an arts organization’s ability to create value through purpose-driven innovations.  This course studies essential elements of creating and maintaining a business of artistic value.

title: Administration in Ensemble Arts
description: Administration in Ensemble Arts explores the organizational and managerial aspects of running successful ensemble programs, both in educational and professional settings. This course covers key topics such as budgeting, scheduling, personnel management, event planning, and promoting ensemble performances. Students will study the principals of how to develop and lead a variety of ensemble types, including choral, instrumental, and mixed groups, while addressing the unique challenges of ensemble coordination.

title: Pedagogy in Ensemble Arts
description: Pedagogy in Ensemble Arts focuses on teaching methodologies and strategies for leading and instructing musical ensembles. The course explores best practices in ensemble rehearsal techniques, musical interpretation, and the development of ensemble cohesion. Students will learn how to teach both individual musicians within the context of an ensemble and manage group dynamics to achieve a unified musical vision.

title: Senior Performance for Non-Music Majors
description: The 25 minute performance will be composed of commercial works of various styles. Final written approval for the recital will be given by the applied instructor. For singers, accompaniment is limited to acoustic piano or guitar, and no sound reinforcement is allowed. Student must be concurrently enrolled in applied study, and must have completed a minimum of eight consecutive semesters of applied study.

title: Research/Creative Project Proposal
description: Development of an individualized original research or creative scholarship project under the mentorship of a faculty member. Tasks will include crafting the proposal, preparing an appropriate review of literature or other background information for the project, and outlining the methods by which the student plans to conduct the project. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course.

title: Research/Creative Project Completion
description: Successful execution of an individualized research or creative scholarship project under the mentorship of a faculty member and consistent with practices in his or her discipline. Students will be evaluated on their adherence to approved methodology, logical development of the project, and time management, as well as their project progress. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course. May be retaken for additional credit if the scope of the research project justifies it.

title: Advanced Digital Audio Production
description: This course provides basic arranging technique for various pop-music genres. Special attention is given to developing arranging technique for rhythm sections, small vocal and instrumental ensemble, studio recordings, and larger vocal and instrumental ensemble. Students complete a major arranging project for presentation in an end of the semester concert setting.

title: Music Programming, Promotion, and Publishing for the Independent Artist
description: Students will become familiar with the responsibilities of a tour planner and a concert promoter. Students will participate in planning concerts in various size venues and learn the procedures and necessary tasks. Students will learn the processes for concert promotion. Tour planning projects are assigned, giving the students insight into the fundamental elements of tour management and production. Booking an artist will also be studied within the context of secular venues, Christian venues, festivals, large and small venues and churches.

title: Research/Creative Project Presentation
description: Revision and presentation of research findings or creative scholarship project developed in 499A & B. Presentation may be submission for publication, oral presentation, or performance for a specified public audience. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course.

title: Music Leadership: Managing for Creativity, Innovation and Collaboration
description: This course identifies and explores leadership principles unique to the field of music. Emphasis is given to the music leader’s responsibility to build team, identify strategic leadership roles in the music industry, cultivate communication skill, develop short- and long-term strategy, resolve conflict and to always function as a music professional. A capstone project to be included in the student’s portfolio is required.

title: Music Leadership Program
description: A capstone performance option for students who elect Music Technology Internship in place of upper division study. This project will consist of producing, recording, and/or engineering a project in a live or studio setting. A minimum of five songs is required. Graded S/U. Every semester. Project Application must be submitted in semester prior to project.

title: Audio for Digital Cinema
description: Designed to teach the student skills in recording, editing and mixing sound for video and film. During the course, students gain hands-on experience through projects implementing key aspects in audio for the digital cinema. Aspects covered include: microphone techniques for recording on-location, sound design, automatic dialogue replacement, Foley, special effects, editing dialogue, sound effects, music score, and mixing in both stereo and surround.

title: Arranging for the Recording Studio
description: Students learn the basics of studio arranging technique as applied to various pop-music genre. Special attention is given to developing arranging technique for rhythm sections, small instrumental or vocal groups and the large choir and orchestra ensemble, all within the recording studio environment. Students complete a major arranging project for performance presentation in an end of the semester recording session.

title: Commercial Music Portfolio
description: An intensive individual production project in which the graduating senior will demonstrate competence in his or her field of commercial music study. A digital portfolio will be created to showcase work done throughout the student's program of study; this will be used for career advancement as well as academic evaluation. Artifacts of commercial music work will be posted in the portfolio that highlights audio/video components and other industry-oriented elements of value - especially online video such as YouTube, song lead sheets, and audio samples, etc. This class will be offered every Spring and students enrolled in the following degrees must register for this class as part of their degree requirements. B.S. in Music Leadership and B.M. in Commercial Music.

title: Senior Performance for Music Majors
description: A 50-minute performance, required of all music majors, comprised of commercial works of various stylistic genres.

title: Commercial Music Internship
description: This course is designed to be a cumulative integration of the student’s learning and experience in a commercial music setting appropriate to the student’s chosen track.

title: Junior Commercial Music Program
description: For the junior commercial performance major. The 25-minute performance will be composed of commercial works of various styles. Final written approval for the performance will be given by the applied instructor. Students must be concurrently enrolled in applied study. For singers, accompaniment is limited to acoustic piano or guitar, and no sound reinforcement is allowed. This is an elective for music students (not necessarily music majors or minors).

title: Songwriting Lessons
description: Private lessons in songwriting. These may replace upper division Applied Study in the Music Liberal Arts (commercial) curriculum.

title: Principles of Marketing
description: Business activities in moving goods from production to final consumption.

title: Consumer Behavior
description: Examination of the consumer decision process in a marketing context. Selected concepts from psychology, sociology, and anthropology are analyzed to develop the student's ability to understand and predict consumer response to the marketing efforts of organizations.

title: Advertising Management
description: Advertising as a function of marketing and merchandising; uses and limitations of advertising as a tool of management; fundamentals in getting a finished advertisement before potential customers; media selection; evaluation criticism and control of advertising.

title: Faculty-Led Academic Research Experiences (FLARE)
description: Intensive participation as a team member in the completion of a scholarly research or creative project. A faculty member will lead each project and will mentor students throughout the project. Enrollment is limited; application is required, and students are selected by the faculty member leading the project. For more information, see the Quality Enhancement Plan section of the Catalog.

title: International Marketing
description: Examines the challenges of entering and operating effectively in foreign markets, managing problems, techniques and strategies needed to apply the marketing concept to the world marketplace, and also enabling the students to perceive international marketing as a managerial challenge. The course will deal with formulation and implementation of international marketing strategies, analysis of the contemporary global marketing environment, marketing mix issues and decisions in international markets, global competitive analysis and strategy, and modification of marketing thinking and practice for foreign markets due to individual environmental differences. Emphasis will be placed on examining the relationship between the international activities of firms and the international, political, legal, and socio-cultural environments prevailing in foreign markets.

title: Internet Marketing
description: Designed to educate and equip students in acquiring basic skills in applying information technologies within the domain of marketing. The course will provide the fundamental knowledge and marketing perspective needed to successfully integrate the internet into the organization's marketing activities. Students will learn the importance of integrating offline and online strategies into the marketing mix for segmenting and targeting consumers. Topics covered include, among others, strategic planning and its tactical implementation in electronic marketing, target market analysis and identification, the internet's marketing capabilities and limitations, marketing channels and digital distribution management of customer and supplier relationships, concerns about privacy and ethics, marketing strategies using social media, the World Wide Web, the different functions and applications of the internet, and the impact of international internet marketing (IIM) on worldwide consumers.

title: Marketing Research
description: Covers the fundamentals of marketing research, as applied to all types of profit and not-for-profit organizations. It focuses on the different types of marketing research (qualitative and quantitative) as well as the complex issues at each stage of the marketing research process, including research objectives, questionnaire construction (specifically for survey research - both traditional and Internet surveys), sampling, data collection, and statistical analysis. Finally, the course discusses responsibilities and issues related to the management of the research function and the use of research information by decision makers faced with a variety of strategic and operational challenges. The skills covered in this course will be applicable to marketing problems encountered in both consumer and business-to-business markets, and public and private sectors.

title: Private Guitar
description: Upper Division applied study offers comprehensive instruction of performance techniques, emphasizing the cultivation of technical prowess and musical proficiency through the study of appropriate repertoire.

title: Coaching the Ensemble Leader
description: This one-on-one coaching course helps students develop ensemble leadership strategies, focusing on interpersonal relationships, pacing, repertoire selection, rehearsal planning, pace and performance, classroom management and contextualization. Students gain practical experience through ensemble work, culminating in a capstone performance. Enrollment in an approved ensemble is required for hands-on learning and laboratory experience.

title: Research/Creative Project Completion
description: Successful execution of an individualized research or creative scholarship project under the mentorship of a faculty member and consistent with practices in his or her discipline. Students will be evaluated on their adherence to approved methodology, logical development of the project, and time management, as well as their project progress. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course. May be retaken for additional credit if the scope of the research project justifies it.

title: Research/Creative Project Proposal
description: Development of an individualized original research or creative scholarship project under the mentorship of a faculty member. Tasks will include crafting the proposal, preparing an appropriate review of literature or other background information for the project, and outlining the methods by which the student plans to conduct the project. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course.

title: Special Problems in Marketing
description: Critical review of the development of retailing thought and important retailing literature. Special and current problems and/or trends in retailing and their impact on the firms and society.

title: Marketing Management
description: Designed to provide an in-depth examination of marketing environments and the impact marketing activities have on organizational operations in competitive, global, multicultural business settings. It discusses both domestic and international frameworks of the fundamental marketing functions of product, pricing, distribution and promotion. Modern marketing problems are explored and analyzed from conceptual, legal, and ethical perspectives and alternatives are developed from a cross-functional perspective. Marketing information systems and the use of advanced technologies in marketing decision-making are also studied.

title: Research/Creative Project Presentation
description: Revision and presentation of research findings or creative scholarship project developed in 499A & B. Presentation may be submission for publication, oral presentation, or performance for a specified public audience. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course.

title: Sales Fundamentals
description: Introduces the basic principles of sales success, sales theory, sales techniques, and role playing. The history of selling is emphasized along with the sales person's role in today's society. The course applies communication theory and principles to the sales situation.

title: Survey Practicum in Music Business
description: Observation and participation in music business companies. Required of music business students and recommended to be taken in the Freshman year after completing MUB 1100. Fifty-five (55) hours of observation are completed from the following areas: record company, music publishing, artist booking agency, music merchandising, music publicity, artist management and music studio. Permission required. Graded S/U.

title: Marketing Technology
description: A skills-based introduction to the basic technology used in creating marketing content including audio and video production as well as design.

title: Music Industry Law
description: A study of the principles and practices of law and management including contract negotiations, torts, crimes, and the relationship of law to the artist and to the market.

title: Field Experience in Music Business
description: Observation, participation, and assisting in music business companies. Required of music business students. Fifty-five (55) hours of field experience are required. Permission required. Graded S/U.

title: Social Media & the Music Industry
description: An introduction to how social media is used to generate discovery and monetization of recorded music and artists.

title: Music Publishing
description: Course deals with the creation, development, exploitation and administration of song copyrights in all genres of music. Areas of focus will include the publisher/songwriter relationship, producing "song" demos, catalog valuation and development, licensing, print, international sub-publishing, sources of income, contracts, legal issues and challenges.

title: The Independent Artist
description: A survey of the process of establishing oneself or supporting someone else as a fully independent artist, often referred to in the music industry as a DIY artist. Includes an introduction to the concepts of production, marketing, distribution, publishing, touring, and the protection of intellectual property

title: Marketing in the Music Industry
description: A study of the movement of the recorded and printed product from the studio to the ultimate consumer. It includes market structure and analysis, distribution, promotion, charts, airplay, and pricing.

title: Concert Management
description: Course deals with the organization of concert promoting, contracts, riders, venues, audience projections and demographics, and finance.

title: Career Internship in Marketing
description: Marketing Service Laboratory (on-the-job experience) under faculty direction. Graded S/U.

title: Artist Management
description: Designed to familiarize the potential manager or music industry executive with the areas of involvement from the artist's perspective and to familiarize the potential artist with the manager's role. Students will learn how to find a manager and when a manager is needed.

title: Faculty-Led Academic Research Experiences (FLARE)
description: Intensive participation as a team member in the completion of a scholarly research or creative project. A faculty member will lead each project and will mentor students throughout the project. Enrollment is limited; application is required, and students are selected by the faculty member leading the project. For more information, see the Quality Enhancement Plan section of the Catalog.

title: Research/Creative Project Completion
description: Successful execution of an individualized research or creative scholarship project under the mentorship of a faculty member and consistent with practices in his or her discipline. Students will be evaluated on their adherence to approved methodology, logical development of the project, and time management, as well as their project progress. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course. May be retaken for additional credit if the scope of the research project justifies it.

title: Research/Creative Project Proposal
description: Development of an individualized original research or creative scholarship project under the mentorship of a faculty member. Tasks will include crafting the proposal, preparing an appropriate review of literature or other background information for the project, and outlining the methods by which the student plans to conduct the project. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course.

title: Survey of Music Business
description: A study of the theoretical foundations of the music industry with special emphasis given to practical applications. An in-depth study of organizations and a general overview of the industry.

title: Operation of a Record Company
description: A detailed overview of the record business from the creation of a record to making it available to consumers. Subjects will include staffing, administration, budgets, legal and business affairs, how performers are signed, production, sales, and distribution.

title: Music Fundamentals
description: A basic theory course designed for the non-music major or as a review for the prospective music major. Covers elementary problems in rhythm, time signatures, scales, intervals and chords.

title: Music Business Internship
description: An opportunity for the student to gain practical experience in music business. The student will apply in the semester prior to the anticipated internship period. The student must secure a faculty sponsor and a sponsoring agency in a field relating to particular specialization. All application forms will be completed and filed no later than one month before the semester during which the internship is to be done. Graded S/U.

title: Finale
description: This course covers the basic operations using Finale music notation software.  Students will learn the various methods of note entry, score setup, adding dynamics, articulations, and other expression markings, editing and formatting, part extraction, exporting audio files, and miscellaneous other skills associated with creating professional-quality musical scores that match academic and industry standards.

title: Public Relations in the Music Industry
description: A course dealing with public relations of the artist, company, and product including: press releases, press kits, press parties, artist kits, news for radio and television, printing bids, and color separation.

title: Art for the Music Major
description: Designed to give students a historical perspective of art, sculpture, and architecture from ancient times to modern times.

title: Recital Attendance
description: Required of all music majors. The graduating music student must have successfully completed a total of 6 semesters for four-year degrees and 3 semesters for two-year degrees. To receive a satisfactory grade, twelve music department-sponsored recitals, concerts, and music majors meetings must be attended during the semester. Graded S/U.

title: Music Appreciation
description: Presents the basic building blocks of music and fosters attentive, intelligent listening to music of different genres and historical periods. Helps the students appreciate, understand, and develop critical thinking skills on selected genres, styles, and periods in the Western European tradition of music as well as examples of music from outside this tradition. Third-party fee required.

title: Class Piano I
description: Beginning class piano. Goals for the semester include the student (1) knowing all major and minor five-finger scales, (2) being able to play major and minor I-V7-1 chord progressions in all keys, and (3) playing prepared pieces that include easy melody and accompaniment. This class is also designed to help prepare the student for MUS 1610.

title: Class Piano II
description: A class designed to follow MUS 1600. Goals for the semester include the student (1) knowing all major and harmonic minor scales (one octave), (2) being able to play appropriate major and minor I-IV-I-V7-I chord progressions in all keys, (3) being able to appropriately harmonize melodies using I, IV, and V7 chords in major and/or minor by ear, and (4) being able to play pieces that are at an easy-to-intermediate level of difficulty. This class is also designed to help prepare the student for MUS 2600.

title: Class Voice I
description: A foundational course with a focus on utilizing the voice as an instrument, vocal health, and applied vocal technique with a focus on performance. Class Voice I is primarily designed for Music and Worship students seeking to fulfill their Secondary Applied requirement.

title: Fine Arts
description: Designed to give students a historical perspective of music, art, sculpture, and architecture from ancient times to modern times.

title: Research/Creative Project Presentation
description: Revision and presentation of research findings or creative scholarship project developed in 499A & B. Presentation may be submission for publication, oral presentation, or performance for a specified public audience. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course.

title: Beginning Class Guitar
description: Designed for the student who has minimal or no experience in guitar. It will cover the fundamental concepts of guitar techniques.

title: Intermediate Class Guitar
description: Designed to further the playing ability of the beginning guitar student.

title: Class Guitar I
description: Class Guitar I is designed for Music and Worship students desiring to study guitar as their secondary applied instrument and develop the foundational skills needed to successfully perform in a live musical setting. The Course will cover the fundamental concepts of guitar techniques, music reading (guitar tablature & music notation), basic open chords, strumming and basic fingerpicking, key Signatures, the major and natural minor scales, notes on the fretboard up to the fifth fret, basic chord chart and Nashville number chart realization.

title: Class Voice II
description: A foundational course continuing instruction utilizing the voice as an instrument, vocal health and applied vocal technique with a focus on performance. Class Voice II is primarily designed for Music and Worship students seeking to fulfill their Secondary Applied requirement.

title: Class Guitar II
description: Class Guitar II is designed for Music and Worship students desiring to study guitar as their secondary applied instrument and develop the foundational skills needed to successfully perform in a live musical setting. The course will cover intermediate guitar concepts of strumming and right-hand finger picking patterns, barre chords, advanced chords and their application in songs, modal theory, the pentatonic scale, transposition and chart reading in both standard notation and Nashville Numbers, continued mastery of the guitar fretboard.

title: Elective Private Voice
description: Elective private study offers comprehensive instruction of performance techniques, emphasizing the cultivation of technical prowess and musical proficiency through the study of appropriate repertoire.

title: Applied Lessons in Music Notation Technology
description: Applied lessons in creating musical scores of various types.  Project goals are commensurate with the skill level of the student, and may include the recreation of existing print music to learn the operations of the program, creating original scores and arrangements, and/or applying notation program(s) within Digital Audio Workstation platform.

description: Lessons for students interested in non-major lessons in conducting. Beat patterns, basic terminology, and basic skills will be covered.

title: Elective Private Composition
description: Private lessons in composition for non-degree seeking students.

title: Choral Union
description: Open to students and Nashville community. No audition is required, but consent of the director is needed. Larger choral works are performed (Messiah, Elijah, Requiem, etc.) with full orchestra accompaniment. Required ensemble of all vocal and keyboard music majors and minors studying applied.

title: Concert Choir
description: The Concert Choir will be a mixed choir that offers a singing experience to students, staff, and community members from every area of Trevecca life. This entry-level ensemble will be non-auditioned and will seek to instill the necessary musicianship skills of aural and notational music literacy, ensembleship, and vocal development necessary to audition into higher levels of the Trevecca choral music program. The repertoire will consist of music from various historical eras of the western cannon as well as traditional and contemporary repertoire throughout the world.

title: Concert Choir: Aesthetics
description: The Concert Choir will be a mixed choir that offers a singing experience to students, staff, and community members from every area of Trevecca life. This entry-level ensemble will be non-auditioned and will seek to instill the necessary musicianship skills of aural and notational music literacy, ensembleship, and vocal development necessary to audition into higher levels of the Trevecca choral music program. The repertoire will consist of music from various historical eras of the western canon as well as traditional and contemporary repertoire throughout the world. This course partially satisfies the World Aesthetics requirement of the General Education Curriculum.

title: TNU Singers
description: A small mixed choral ensemble open to all students by audition.

title: Elective Private Percussion
description: Elective private study offers comprehensive instruction of performance techniques, emphasizing the cultivation of technical prowess and musical proficiency through the study of appropriate repertoire.

title: Wind Ensemble
description: Open to all students. It provides opportunity for continued development of instrumental performance ability. A balanced repertoire from both sacred and secular literature is performed in concerts given throughout the year. All who wish to participate must be approved by the director. Required of instrumental majors.

title: Elective Private Guitar
description: Elective private study offers comprehensive instruction of performance techniques, emphasizing the cultivation of technical prowess and musical proficiency through the study of appropriate repertoire.

title: Trevecca Chorale Aesthetics
description: The Trevecca Chorale is an auditioned mixed choir, specializing in the great sacred choral repertoire of all periods of music history and contemporary compositions, including both a cappella and accompanied works. Membership is open to all students regardless of major. Three separate completions of this course will satisfy the World Aesthetics requirement of the Liberal Arts curriculum of the baccalaureate degree.

title: TNU Singers: Aesthetics
description: A small mixed choral ensemble, open to all students by audition. Music of the Renaissance and Baroque eras is featured. This course partially satisfies the World Aesthetics requirement of the General Education Curriculum.

title: Jazz Ensemble
description: The Jazz Ensemble, or TNaz Jazz, is a traditional big band that plays music from the Big Band era to the present.  Open to all students by permission of director.

title: Jazz Ensemble: Aesthetics
description: An ensemble focusing on Jazz/Commercial Music of the 20th and 21st Centuries, open to all students by audition. This course partially satisfies the World Aesthetics requirement of the General Education Curriculum.

title: Wind Ensemble: Aesthetics
description: Open to all students. It provides opportunity for continued development of instrumental performance ability. A balanced repertoire from both sacred and secular literature is performed in concerts given throughout the year. All who wish to participate must be approved by the director. This course partially satisfies the World Aesthetics requirement of the General Education Curriculum.

title: Chamber Music Ensemble
description: Provides opportunity for participation in ensembles of a more intimate nature. Open to all students by audition.

title: Trevecca Symphony Orchestra
description: Open to students and Nashville community by audition. Continuous openings for strings and occasional openings for winds and percussion. Opportunities to perform stimulating symphonic orchestral and string literature on campus and beyond. Required ensemble of all string majors and minors.

title: Guitar Ensemble
description: Performs a diverse range of guitar repertoire on acoustic instruments. Students must be able to read music.

title: Trevecca Chorale
description: The Trevecca Chorale is an auditioned mixed choir, specializing in the great sacred choral repertoire of all periods of music history and contemporary compositions, including both a cappella and accompanied works. Membership is open to all students regardless of major.

title: Woodwind Ensemble
description: Flexible instrumentation. Performs standard Woodwind Quintet, Quartet or Trio literature as well as sacred pieces.

title: Brass Ensemble
description: Performs standard brass quintet literature and some sacred pieces.

title: Percussion Ensemble
description: Performs a diverse range of percussion ensemble repertoire.

title: TNU New Music Ensemble
description: This is an elective ensemble that specializes in the creation and performance of new music.

title: Lyric Theatre Ensemble
description: A performance class in which students prepare a variety of operatic and musical theatre scenes. Attention will be given to movement, diction, acting, singing, and vocal interpretation. The class will culminate in a staged performance.

title: Trevecca Symphony Orchestra: Aesthetics
description: Open to students and Nashville community by audition. Continuous openings for strings and occasional openings for winds and percussion. Opportunities to perform stimulating symphonic orchestral and string literature on campus and beyond. This course partially satisfies the World Aesthetics requirement of the General Education Curriculum.

title: Theory I
description: This course teaches music fundamentals (pitch, rhythm, chords, scales, intervals) and basic form/analysis. All assignments have a written, aural, piano, and analysis application.

title: Theory II
description: This course is a continuation of MUS 2010. Students investigate intermediate music fundamentals (pitch, rhythm, chords, scales, intervals) and basic form/analysis. All assignments include a written, aural, piano, and analysis application.

title: Aural Theory I
description: The development of music notation literacy and musicianship skills through sight-singing, ear training, and tonal/rhythm dictation in varying meters and modalities.

title: Jazz Combo
description: A jazz chamber ensemble that emphasizes the performance of historical literature and improvisation.

title: Aural Theory II
description: Continuation of Aural Theory I. Further development of music notation literacy and musicianship skills through sight-singing, ear training, and tonal/rhythm dictation in varying meters and modalities.

title: Band Arranging
description: Arranging for concert band and wind ensemble. The course will include writing assignments for various combinations of instruments and provide the student with a broad overview of arranging music for ensembles of various skill levels. Coursework will include studying conventions and techniques applied by various arrangers and composers, as well as a brief introduction to band literature through score study and recordings.

title: Music Notation Technology II
description: This course is an overview in comparing various music notation programs.  Students will gain knowledge in the application of each program as it pertains to creating publisher-quality electronic notation.  Advanced concepts may include combining one or more notation programs with Digital Audio Workstation software for multimedia applications.

title: Brass Methods
description: A study of teaching problems through the practical experience of learning to play brass under supervision. Development of fundamental techniques and acquaintance with literature for the beginning student.

title: Woodwind Methods
description: A study of teaching problems through the practical experience of learning to play woodwinds under supervision. Development of fundamental techniques and acquaintance with literature for the beginning student.

title: Marching Band
description: Provides instrumental students with an opportunity to participate in the Vanderbilt Marching Band. Permission of the instrumental instructor is required. At least one hour is required of all instrumental music education majors.

title: Percussion Methods
description: A study of teaching problems through the practical experience of learning to play percussion under supervision. Development of fundamental techniques and acquaintance with literature for the beginning student.

title: History I
description: Study of the development of Western music from ancient civilization through the Renaissance.

title: History II
description: Study of the development of Western music during the Baroque and Classical periods.

title: World Music and Culture
description: A study of the development of indigenous music of Eastern and Western cultures and how that music is both a product of and a contributor to those cultures. The class will be taught with an anthropological approach that views each particular culture through the lens of the music that each culture produces.

title: Class Piano III
description: A class designed to follow MUS 1610. Goals for the semester include the student (1) knowing all major and harmonic minor scales, (2) being able to play appropriate major and minor I-IV-I-V7/V-V7-I chord progressions in all keys, (3) being able to appropriately harmonize melodies using I, IV, V7/V, and V7 chords in major and/or minor by ear, (4) being able to sight-read simple songs, and (5) being able to play pieces that are at an intermediate level of difficulty. This class is also designed to help prepare the student for MUS 2620.

title: Elementary and Preschool Music Teaching Methodology
description: Examines the dominant music teaching methods for young children including Orff, Kodaly, Dalcroze, and Gordon's "Music Learning Theory" and introduces students to the basic elements of teaching music to children.

title: Theory III
description: Continuation of Theory II. The study of music from 1800 to 1900.

title: Theory IV
description: Continuation of Theory III. The study of music from 1900 to the present with emphasis on non-traditional forms of composition.

title: Instrument Repair
description: Deals with the methods, materials, and skills needed in repairing musical instruments of all kinds. Students will be required to disassemble and reassemble instruments and diagnose and correct problems in malfunctioning instruments. Fee charged.

description: Continuation of Aural Theory III. Further development of music notation literacy and musicianship skills through sight-singing, ear training, and tonal/rhythm dictation in varying meters and modalities.

title: Elementary Conducting
description: A study of the fundamentals of conducting. Emphasis on beat patterns and methods of indicating meter, tempo, volume and style. Material drawn from sacred and secular choral music. Open to all students. Required of all majors and minors.

title: Advanced Conducting
description: Continuation of MUS 3100 with emphasis on baton skills. Materials will be drawn from appropriate instrumental and instrumental/ choral combination literature.

title: Secondary Vocal Music Teaching Methodology
description: A course for pre-professional music educators and future traditional choral conductors to gain tools for teaching choral music in a secondary school setting. Course topics include teaching singing, aural and notational literacy, and choral music performance in a group context.

title: Secondary Instrumental Methods I
description: An introduction to the skills, methods and materials needed to become a successful secondary school instrumental director. This class will cover subjects ranging from refining an educational philosophy to knowing the fingerings of the clarinet.

title: Private Harp
description: Lower Division applied study offers comprehensive instruction of performance techniques, emphasizing the cultivation of technical prowess and musical proficiency through the study of appropriate repertoire.

title: Faculty-Led Academic Research Experiences (FLARE)
description: Intensive participation as a team member in the completion of a scholarly research or creative project. A faculty member will lead each project and will mentor students throughout the project. Enrollment is limited; application is required, and students are selected by the faculty member leading the project. For more information, see the Quality Enhancement Plan section of the Catalog.

title: Counterpoint
description: Covers the rudiments of species counterpoint in two-, three-, and four-part textures. Emphasis is on score study, four-part chorale writing, canon, and fugue.

title: History III
description: Study of the development of Western music during the Romantic period.

title: History IV
description: Study of the development of Western music during the 20th century and beyond.

title: Aural Theory III
description: Continuation of Aural Theory II. Further development of music notation literacy and musicianship skills through sight-singing, ear training, and tonal/rhythm dictation in varying meters and modalities.

title: Music Therapy I
description: A broad introduction to the concepts, terminology, primary applications, career tracks and connections between the field of Psychology and Music Therapy.

title: Keyboard Proficiency
description: Students master and are evaluated on the foundational skills required to successfully complete the piano proficiency via successfully completing the required assigned quizzes (30)

title: Diction for Singers I
description: This course is designed to develop pronunciation skills for speaking and singing in English and Italian, particularly through use of the International Phonetic Alphabet and the rules of grammar that affect pronunciation for poetry and song literature text.

title: Junior Recital
description: Recital for the junior performance major. The 25 minute recital will be composed of classical works from various style periods. Final written approval for the recital will be given by the applied instructor. Student must be concurrently enrolled in applied study. This is an elective for music students (not necessarily music majors or minors). Presentation of a junior recital does not waive the jury requirement. The 0-hour option to be graded S/U.

title: Diction for Singers II
description: A continuation of MUS 3620 Diction for Singers I, this course is designed to develop pronunciation skills for speaking and singing in German and French, particularly through use of the International Phonetic Alphabet and the rules of grammar that affect pronunciation for poetry and song literature text.

title: Secondary Instrumental Methods II
description: A continuation of the study of the skills, methods, and materials needed to become a successful secondary school instrumental director. This class will cover subjects ranging from refining an educational philosophy to knowing the fingerings of the clarinet.

title: Research/Creative Project Completion
description: Successful execution of an individualized research or creative scholarship project under the mentorship of a faculty member and consistent with practices in his or her discipline. Students will be evaluated on their adherence to approved methodology, logical development of the project, and time management, as well as their project progress. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course. May be retaken for additional credit if the scope of the research project justifies it.

title: Research/Creative Project Proposal
description: Development of an individualized original research or creative scholarship project under the mentorship of a faculty member. Tasks will include crafting the proposal, preparing an appropriate review of literature or other background information for the project, and outlining the methods by which the student plans to conduct the project. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course.

title: Research/Creative Project Presentation
description: Revision and presentation of research findings or creative scholarship project developed in 499A & B. Presentation may be submission for publication, oral presentation, or performance for a specified public audience. Permission of faculty-mentor and department chair or dean is required before student registers for the course.

title: Orchestration
description: A practical study of the techniques of arranging for various types of instrumental groups.

title: Choral Arranging
description: Students learn basic choral arranging technique as applied to various vocal genre and musical style. Special attention is given to developing competency in arranging for small and large vocal ensemble and, in a variety of performance settings.  Students prepare an end of the semester choral arrangement specifically for in-class, public or recording studio presentation.

title: Keyboard Literature and Pedagogy
description: Designed to familiarize the student with selected keyboard literature from the Baroque to the present. Discussion and study of selected teaching methods and materials is included.

title: Music Therapy II
description: A continuation of the study of the concepts, terminology, primary applications, career tracks and connections between the field of Psychology and Music Therapy.

title: Woodwind Literature and Pedagogy
description: Involves the theory and practice of teaching applied woodwinds. Includes discussion and study of materials and methods with practical experience of teaching in a lab situation.

title: String Literature and Pedagogy
description: Involves the theory and practice of teaching applied strings. Includes discussion and study of materials and methods with practical experience of teaching in a lab situation.

title: Percussion Literature and Pedagogy
description: This class addresses the theory and practice of teaching percussion instruments in the context of private lessons.  The methods and materials used in teaching private percussion will be studied and evaluated.

title: Brass Literature and Pedagogy
description: Involves the theory and practice of teaching applied brass. Includes discussion and study of materials and methods with practical experience of teaching in a lab situation.

title: Senior Recital for Non-Music Majors
description: The 25 minute recital will be composed of classical works from various style periods. Final written approval for the recital will be given by the applied instructor. Student must be concurrently enrolled in applied study, and must have completed a minimum of eight consecutive semesters of applied study. As needed.

title: Vocal Literature
description: This course is designed to survey the development of western solo vocal literature from approximately 1550 to the present, with emphasis on Italian, German, French, English, and American song. Opera and oratorio literature will be discussed as it pertains to the progression of solo song but is not the focus of this course.

title: Directed Study in Music
description: Consists of special projects or specialized research in music.

title: Form and Analysis
description: Explores the analysis of musical forms across the scope of music history to the present.

title: Seminar in Advanced Conducting
description: Advanced study in conducting, majoring on actual conducting experience under faculty supervision. Open only to those who have completed the regular conducting sequence of courses, with consent of the instructor.

title: Senior Recital for Music Majors
description: The 50 minute recital, required of all music majors, will be composed of classical works from various style periods. The candidate must perform a representative portion of the recital from memory (for voice majors) before the voice/choral faculty (voice majors) or the instrumental faculty (instrumental majors) one month preceding the scheduled public performance. The student must be concurrently enrolled in private study for 2 credit hours. Graded S/U.

description: For upper-division students who desire to explore special aspects of music literature, history, performance or a music-related topic such as music therapy, or recording studio.
